news_summary: Advancements in clean energy and sustainable agriculture are accelerating with improved lithium extraction for EV batteries, solar power surpassing coal in Texas, extended mango freshness through temperature control, and successful restoration of rare coastal grasslands.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès dans les domaines des énergies propres et de l'agriculture durable s'accélèrent, avec notamment l'amélioration des techniques d'extraction du lithium pour les batteries des véhicules électriques, le dépassement du charbon par l'énergie solaire au Texas, la prolongation de la durée de conservation des mangues grâce au contrôle de la température et la restauration réussie de prairies côtières rares.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de energía limpia y agricultura sostenible se están acelerando gracias a la mejora de la extracción de litio para las baterías de los vehículos eléctricos, al hecho de que la energía solar haya superado al carbón en Texas, a la prolongación de la frescura del mango mediante el control de la temperatura y a la restauración satisfactoria de praderas costeras poco comunes.

articles.title: Scientists just found a faster, cleaner way to extract lithium for EV batteries
articles.summary: A breakthrough lithium-extraction method could help solve one of clean energy’s dirtiest problems. Researchers at Columbia Engineering have developed a fast new technique that pulls lithium directly from salty underground brines using a temperature-sensitive solvent, avoiding the giant evaporation ponds that can take years and drain precious water supplies. Even better, the method works on low-quality lithium sources that current technologies struggle to use.
articles.content:
Demand for lithium is soaring as automakers ramp up electric vehicle production and energy companies build larger battery systems to support wind and solar power. But producing lithium remains a slow and environmentally costly process. Current extraction methods work best with high quality deposits found in only a limited number of regions, while also consuming huge amounts of land and water.

Now, researchers at Columbia Engineering have developed a new lithium extraction technique that could speed up production, reduce pollution, and tap reserves that existing technologies struggle to access.

Their findings, published in the journal Joule, describe a process called switchable solvent selective extraction, or S3E (pronounced S three E). The method uses a temperature responsive solvent to pull lithium directly from salty underground brines, even when lithium concentrations are low or mixed with other minerals that are difficult to separate.

New Lithium Extraction Method Shows Strong Selectivity

According to the research team, S3E demonstrated impressive selectivity during testing. The system extracted lithium at rates up to 10 times higher than sodium and 12 times higher than potassium. It also removed magnesium, one of the most common contaminants in lithium brines, through a chemical precipitation step that separates the unwanted material.

Unlike many current direct lithium extraction systems, S3E does not depend on specialized binding chemicals or large amounts of postprocessing. Instead, it relies on the unique way lithium ions interact with water molecules inside a solvent that changes behavior depending on temperature.

At room temperature, the solvent absorbs lithium and water from the brine. Once heated, the system releases purified lithium and water while regenerating the solvent so it can be reused repeatedly.

Why Current Lithium Production Is a Problem

About 40% of the world's lithium supply comes from salty underground brines located beneath desert regions. Most producers rely on solar evaporation, a process that pumps brine into enormous outdoor ponds and leaves it exposed to the sun for months or even years until enough water evaporates.

This approach is heavily dependent on dry climates, flat terrain, and vast stretches of land, making it practical only in select places such as Chile's Atacama Desert and parts of Nevada. It also requires significant water use in already water stressed regions.

"There's no way solar evaporation alone can match future demand," said Ngai Yin Yip, La Von Duddleson Krumb Associate Professor of Earth and Environmental Engineering at Columbia University. "And there are promising lithium-rich brines, like those in California's Salton Sea, where this method simply can't be used at all."

Salton Sea Lithium Could Power Millions of EV Batteries

To test the system, researchers used synthetic brines designed to mimic conditions at California's Salton Sea, a geothermal region believed to contain enough lithium to supply more than 375 million EV batteries.

After four extraction cycles using the same solvent batch, the team recovered nearly 40% of the lithium. The results suggest the technology could eventually support continuous large scale operation.

"This is a new way to do direct lithium extraction," said Yip. "It's fast, selective, and easy to scale. And it can be powered by low-grade heat from waste sources or solar collectors."

The researchers emphasized that the project is still at the proof of concept stage and has not yet been fully optimized for efficiency or maximum lithium recovery. Even so, they believe S3E could become a viable alternative to evaporation ponds and hard rock mining, which currently dominate global lithium production despite their environmental drawbacks.

Cleaner Lithium Production for the Clean Energy Transition

As demand for batteries continues to rise worldwide, cleaner lithium extraction technologies may become increasingly important for the clean energy transition.

"We talk about green energy all the time," said Yip. "But we rarely talk about how dirty some of the supply chains are. If we want a truly sustainable transition, we need cleaner ways to get the materials it depends on. This is one step in that direction."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in lithium extraction that addresses major environmental challenges associated with current lithium production methods. The new method is faster, cleaner, uses less water, and can access previously unusable lithium sources, which has broad implications for sustainable energy and electric vehicle battery production worldwide. The article provides detailed context on the problem, the innovation, and its potential large-scale impact, meeting all criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New method speeds up and cleans lithium extraction for EV batteries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Lithium is a key material used in batteries for electric vehicles (EVs) and renewable energy storage. Right now, most lithium comes from salty underground water called brine, but the usual way to get it is slow and uses a lot of water and land. This causes environmental problems, especially in dry areas.

**What happened** – Scientists at Columbia University created a new way to pull lithium from salty brines using a special liquid called a temperature-sensitive solvent. This method, called S3E, works faster than current methods and can get lithium even when it’s mixed with other minerals. It uses heat to release lithium and then reuses the solvent, making it cleaner and more efficient.

**Impact** – This new method can avoid large evaporation ponds that take years and waste water. It can also work with lithium sources that are hard to use now, like those under California’s Salton Sea, which could supply millions of EV batteries. The process is faster, uses less water, and can reduce pollution, making lithium production less harmful to the environment.

**What's next step** – The researchers still need to improve the method to get even more lithium and make it work on a larger scale. If successful, it could replace older, dirtier ways of lithium extraction and help meet the growing demand for batteries in electric vehicles and clean energy systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new temperature-based solvent method offers a faster, cleaner way to extract lithium from salty brines, promising better battery materials for electric vehicles with less environmental harm.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode accélère et nettoie l’extraction du lithium pour batteries électriques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le lithium est un matériau essentiel pour les batteries des véhicules électriques (VE) et le stockage d’énergie renouvelable. Aujourd’hui, la plupart du lithium provient d’eaux salées souterraines appelées saumures, mais la méthode habituelle est lente et consomme beaucoup d’eau et d’espace, ce qui pose des problèmes environnementaux, surtout dans les régions sèches.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’université de Columbia ont créé une nouvelle technique pour extraire le lithium des saumures en utilisant un liquide spécial sensible à la température. Cette méthode, appelée S3E, est plus rapide que les méthodes actuelles et peut extraire le lithium même lorsqu’il est mélangé à d’autres minéraux. Elle utilise la chaleur pour libérer le lithium et réutilise ensuite le solvant, ce qui la rend plus propre et efficace.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode évite les grands bassins d’évaporation qui prennent des années et gaspillent de l’eau. Elle fonctionne aussi avec des sources de lithium difficiles à exploiter aujourd’hui, comme celles sous la Salton Sea en Californie, qui pourraient fournir des millions de batteries pour VE. Ce procédé est plus rapide, consomme moins d’eau et réduit la pollution, rendant la production de lithium moins nuisible pour l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs doivent encore améliorer la méthode pour extraire plus de lithium et la rendre utilisable à grande échelle. Si cela réussit, elle pourrait remplacer les anciennes méthodes plus polluantes et répondre à la demande croissante en batteries pour véhicules électriques et systèmes d’énergie propre.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode utilisant un solvant sensible à la température offre une extraction plus rapide et plus propre du lithium à partir des saumures, promettant des matériaux de batterie meilleurs pour les véhicules électriques avec un impact environnemental réduit.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método acelera y limpia la extracción de litio para baterías de vehículos eléctricos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El litio es un material clave para las baterías de vehículos eléctricos (VE) y para almacenar energía renovable. Actualmente, la mayoría del litio proviene de aguas saladas subterráneas llamadas salmueras, pero la forma habitual de extraerlo es lenta y consume mucha agua y espacio, causando problemas ambientales, especialmente en zonas secas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Columbia desarrollaron una nueva técnica que usa un líquido especial sensible a la temperatura para sacar litio de las salmueras. Este método, llamado S3E, es más rápido que los actuales y puede extraer litio incluso cuando está mezclado con otros minerales. Usa calor para liberar el litio y luego reutiliza el solvente, haciendo el proceso más limpio y eficiente.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método evita los grandes estanques de evaporación que tardan años y desperdician agua. También puede trabajar con fuentes de litio que ahora son difíciles de usar, como las bajo el Salton Sea en California, que podrían alimentar millones de baterías para VE. El proceso es más rápido, usa menos agua y reduce la contaminación, haciendo que la producción de litio sea menos dañina para el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores aún deben mejorar el método para extraer más litio y hacerlo funcionar a gran escala. Si tiene éxito, podría reemplazar métodos antiguos y más contaminantes, ayudando a satisfacer la creciente demanda de baterías para vehículos eléctricos y energía limpia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo método con un solvente sensible a la temperatura ofrece una forma más rápida y limpia de extraer litio de salmueras, prometiendo mejores materiales para baterías de vehículos eléctricos con menos daño ambiental.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene desert landscape with gently steaming underground brine pools glowing softly, where a translucent, temperature-responsive solvent swirls around glowing lithium ions being selectively drawn upward; nearby, stylized solar collectors and subtle heat waves symbolize clean energy powering the process, all rendered in natural, earthy tones with soft blues and warm amber highlights.

articles.title: Solar to overtake coal on Texas grid for the first time ever this year
articles.content:
This story was originally published by Canary Media and is reproduced here as part of the Climate Desk collaboration.

The Texas sun keeps rising, as Texas coal wanes.

For the first time ever, solar is set to generate more electricity than coal in the power market managed by the Electric Reliability Council of Texas, or ERCOT. Nobody is building new coal power plants in the state, but developers are adding more solar there than anywhere else in the country. As a result of those diverging trajectories, the federal government expects ERCOT will receive 78 billion kilowatt-hours from solar in 2026 and just 60 from coal.

This trend does have seasonal variations. Last year, solar output beat coal on a monthly basis from March through August, and this year it is expected to do so from March through December, per the U.S. Energy Information Administration, or EIA, at the Department of Energy.

Nationally, the combination of wind and solar surpassed coal generation in 2024, as noted in an analysis by Ember, a think tank that conducts research on clean energy. In other words, the solar industry is further along in Texas than it is nationwide.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The Texas solar surge undercuts the prevailing energy narratives coming out of the Trump administration, which has attempted to boost coal and gas as tools of ​“energy dominance,” while blocking or canceling American energy that comes from renewables. The Department of Energy, for instance, is keeping struggling coal plants on life support at great expense to taxpayers. Meanwhile, the Department of the Interior is blocking wind and solar developments that intersect with public lands.

Trump officials have argued that coal is more reliable than solar because it can generate power around the clock. But even with that advantage, coal plants in Texas can’t keep up with the total annual and monthly production from the rapidly growing solar fleet. This has not damaged grid reliability, because ERCOT meets evening demand with a diverse portfolio, including gas plants, nuclear, wind, and, increasingly, batteries, which store all that excess solar power for use when the sun stops shining.

Of course, Texas leaders did not set out to disprove the Trump administration’s energy claims. The maverick Lone Star State kept its electricity system out of the hands of federal regulators, and in the 1990s and early 2000s reformed it to promote free market competition instead of centralized planning by monopoly utilities. That market, coupled with lots of space and lax building regulations, has made an ideal environment for wind, solar, and batteries to flourish. Now, Texas is fortified with tens of gigawatts of new capacity with which to tackle heat waves and temper price spikes.

Deep-red Texas offers lessons for the liberal states that have committed to lofty climate goals yet failed to build much solar or batteries so far. They can’t immediately switch over to an ERCOT-style market, but they can take steps to speed up the time it takes to get permits and grid connection, dial back the level of deference to habitually conservative legacy utilities, and make sure that clean energy gets a fair shot in the race to serve surging energy needs. And it’s always a good time to reexamine old market rules that subtly privilege entrenched players at the expense of new entrants that would make cheaper and cleaner power.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

After more of the rapid-fire solar buildout, EIA expects ERCOT will produce 99 billion kilowatt-hours of solar power in 2027, up 27 percent from 2026. At that point, the upstart industry will have left its well-established coal competition in the dust.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant milestone where solar power generation in Texas is set to surpass coal for the first time, indicating a major shift towards renewable energy with broad environmental and societal benefits. It provides detailed context on the scale, implications for energy markets, and the positive impact on grid reliability and climate goals, making it a substantial and inspiring development in clean energy.
articles.personality_title: Solar power set to surpass coal in Texas electricity grid for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Texas has long relied on coal for electricity, but solar energy has been growing quickly. The state’s power system is run by the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT), which manages most of Texas’s electricity supply.

**What happened** – For the first time, solar energy is expected to produce more electricity than coal in Texas. By 2026, solar power is projected to generate 78 billion kilowatt-hours, while coal will generate only 60 billion kilowatt-hours. This shift is due to new solar projects being built and no new coal plants being developed.

**Impact** – This change shows that solar energy can compete with and even beat coal in a large energy market. Despite earlier ideas that coal is more reliable because it works all day and night, solar power combined with wind, gas, nuclear, and batteries keeps the Texas grid steady. This means cleaner energy can be reliable and affordable.

**What’s next step** – Solar power in Texas is expected to keep growing fast. By 2027, solar production may reach 99 billion kilowatt-hours, widening the gap with coal. Other states might learn from Texas’s approach to encourage more clean energy by speeding up permits and changing old rules.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, Texas will generate more electricity from solar power than from coal, marking a major step forward for renewable energy in a big U.S. state.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’énergie solaire devrait dépasser le charbon sur le réseau texan pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Texas a longtemps dépendu du charbon pour son électricité, mais l’énergie solaire se développe rapidement. Le système électrique de l’État est géré par le Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT), qui supervise la plupart de l’électricité au Texas.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour la première fois, on prévoit que l’énergie solaire produira plus d’électricité que le charbon au Texas. En 2026, l’énergie solaire devrait générer 78 milliards de kilowattheures, contre seulement 60 milliards pour le charbon. Cette évolution vient des nouveaux projets solaires construits et de l’absence de nouvelles centrales au charbon.

**Impact** – Ce changement montre que l’énergie solaire peut rivaliser avec le charbon et même le dépasser sur un grand marché de l’énergie. Malgré l’idée que le charbon est plus fiable car il fonctionne jour et nuit, le solaire, combiné à l’éolien, au gaz, au nucléaire et aux batteries, maintient la stabilité du réseau texan. Cela signifie que l’énergie propre peut être fiable et abordable.

**Prochaine étape** – L’énergie solaire au Texas devrait continuer à croître rapidement. En 2027, la production solaire pourrait atteindre 99 milliards de kilowattheures, creusant l’écart avec le charbon. D’autres États pourraient s’inspirer du modèle texan en accélérant les permis et en révisant les anciennes règles.

**En une phrase** – Pour la première fois, le Texas produira plus d’électricité grâce au solaire qu’au charbon, marquant une avancée importante pour les énergies renouvelables dans un grand État américain.
articles.personality_title_es: La energía solar superará al carbón en la red eléctrica de Texas por primera vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Texas ha dependido durante mucho tiempo del carbón para la electricidad, pero la energía solar está creciendo rápidamente. El sistema eléctrico del estado es gestionado por el Consejo de Confiabilidad Eléctrica de Texas (ERCOT), que controla la mayor parte del suministro eléctrico en Texas.

**Qué pasó** – Por primera vez, se espera que la energía solar produzca más electricidad que el carbón en Texas. Para 2026, se proyecta que la energía solar generará 78 mil millones de kilovatios-hora, mientras que el carbón solo 60 mil millones. Este cambio se debe a la construcción de nuevos proyectos solares y a que no se están desarrollando nuevas plantas de carbón.

**Impacto** – Este cambio muestra que la energía solar puede competir y superar al carbón en un gran mercado energético. Aunque antes se pensaba que el carbón era más confiable porque funciona día y noche, la energía solar combinada con viento, gas, nuclear y baterías mantiene estable la red de Texas. Esto demuestra que la energía limpia puede ser confiable y asequible.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que la energía solar en Texas siga creciendo rápidamente. Para 2027, la producción solar podría alcanzar 99 mil millones de kilovatios-hora, aumentando la diferencia con el carbón. Otros estados podrían aprender del modelo de Texas para fomentar más energía limpia acelerando permisos y cambiando reglas antiguas.

**Una frase clave** – Por primera vez, Texas generará más electricidad con energía solar que con carbón, marcando un paso importante para las energías renovables en un gran estado de EE. UU.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a vast Texas landscape bathed in warm sunlight, featuring expansive rows of gleaming solar panels stretching toward the horizon, with a fading, rusted coal power plant silhouette in the background being gently overtaken by the bright solar array, under a clear sky with soft, natural colors emphasizing renewal and clean energy progress.

articles.title: Scientists discover the perfect temperature to keep mangoes fresh much longer
articles.summary: Scientists found that storing mangoes at 54°F dramatically slows ripening and keeps the fruit fresh far longer than typical tropical temperatures. The cooler conditions helped mangoes stay firm, retain moisture, and preserve important antioxidants while reducing cellular damage. Researchers also uncovered the internal defense systems that switch on during cold storage, protecting the fruit from stress and decay.
articles.content:
Mangoes are among the world's most popular tropical fruits, valued for their sweet flavor and rich nutritional content. But once harvested, mangoes continue to ripen quickly, making them highly vulnerable to softening, moisture loss, and spoilage during storage and transport.

Now, researchers from Hainan University have uncovered why storing mangoes at 12°C (54°F) dramatically extends freshness without causing cold damage. Their study, published in Tropical Plants, found that the cooler temperature slows ripening, preserves fruit structure, and activates the mango's natural antioxidant defenses.

The findings could help improve cold-chain transportation for tropical fruits while reducing waste and extending shelf life.

How Temperature Changes Mango Ripening

In many tropical regions, mangoes are commonly transported at temperatures between 26°C (79°F) and 30°C (86°F). While convenient, these warmer conditions speed up respiration and ripening, causing fruit to soften and deteriorate more rapidly.

Scientists have long known that colder storage can slow this process. However, tropical fruits such as mangoes are sensitive to chilling injury when temperatures become too low. Earlier observations suggested that 12°C worked well for preserving 'Tainong No.1' mangoes, but researchers did not fully understand the biological reasons behind its effectiveness.

To investigate, the research team compared mangoes stored at 12°C with fruit stored at 30°C over a 24 day period.

What Researchers Measured

The scientists used a wide range of tests to track how storage temperature affected mango quality. They monitored fruit color, firmness, weight loss, sugar content, acidity, respiration rate, and levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are unstable molecules linked to cellular damage.

The study also examined antioxidant compounds such as vitamin C, phenolics, and flavonoids. Researchers measured the activity of protective enzymes including APX, SOD, PAL, and POD, while also analyzing gene expression linked to antioxidant defense systems.

Microscopic imaging allowed the team to examine changes inside mango pulp cells over time.

Cooler Storage Preserved Mango Quality

During the first 12 days, mangoes stored at 12°C and 30°C looked fairly similar. After day 16, however, major differences began to appear.

Fruit stored at 30°C yellowed rapidly, while mangoes kept at 12°C maintained their color longer because chlorophyll breakdown slowed significantly. Sugar levels increased quickly in the warmer fruit before declining, while mangoes stored at 12°C showed a slower and steadier rise.

Acidity also disappeared much faster at 30°C. Meanwhile, mangoes kept at 12°C retained significantly more acidity, helping preserve flavor balance.

The physical condition of the fruit showed even larger contrasts. Mangoes stored at 30°C lost more than 17% of their weight, while fruit stored at 12°C lost less than 4%. Firmness also declined far more slowly under cooler storage conditions.

Researchers observed major structural differences at the cellular level. Mangoes kept at 12°C retained intact cell walls and starch granules even after 24 days. By comparison, fruit stored at 30°C showed early thinning of cell walls, starch depletion, and eventual cell collapse.

Antioxidant Defenses Help Mangoes Resist Spoilage

The study found that 12°C storage reduced the buildup of malondialdehyde (MDA) and reactive oxygen species, both associated with oxidative stress and cellular deterioration.

At the same time, cooler temperatures helped preserve higher levels of vitamin C, phenolics, and flavonoids. Protective antioxidant enzymes remained more active for longer periods in mangoes stored at 12°C.

Gene analysis revealed increased activity in antioxidant-related genes including MiAPX1, MiAPX2, MiSOD1, and MiSOD2. According to the researchers, these genes help strengthen the fruit's natural defense systems and maintain redox balance, reducing cellular damage during storage.

Correlation analysis further showed that higher ROS and MDA levels were linked to greater softening and water loss. In contrast, stronger antioxidant activity was associated with lower oxidative stress and better fruit preservation.

Implications for Mango Shipping and Storage

The findings provide practical guidance for the mango industry and cold-chain logistics systems. Maintaining storage temperatures near 12°C may allow mangoes to be harvested earlier, transported over longer distances, and ripened closer to their final markets while minimizing spoilage and quality loss.

The research was funded by the Hainan Province Agricultural Reclamation Team Joint Innovation Project (Grant No. HKKJ202432), the National Key Research and Development Program Project (Grant No. 2023YFD2300803-7), and Hainan University Mango Industry Technology System Construction Project.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a scientific discovery that significantly extends the freshness of mangoes by identifying an optimal storage temperature, which can reduce food waste and improve cold-chain logistics for tropical fruits. This has broad implications for global food supply chains and sustainability, benefiting consumers and producers alike. The article provides detailed scientific context and practical applications, meeting the criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find ideal temperature to keep mangoes fresh longer
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mangoes are popular tropical fruits known for their sweet taste and nutrition. However, they ripen quickly after harvest, which makes them spoil fast during storage and transport.

**What happened** – Researchers from Hainan University discovered that storing mangoes at 12°C (54°F) slows down ripening and keeps the fruit fresh for much longer without causing cold damage. They studied mangoes stored at this temperature compared to those kept at typical tropical temperatures around 30°C (86°F) over 24 days.

**Impact** – The cooler temperature helped mangoes stay firm, keep moisture, and preserve important antioxidants like vitamin C. It also activated the fruit’s natural defense systems, protecting cells from damage and slowing softening and color changes. This means mangoes can be shipped farther and stored longer without losing quality.

**What’s next step** – These findings can improve how mangoes and other tropical fruits are transported and stored worldwide. The mango industry may adopt 12°C storage to reduce waste and deliver fresher fruit to markets, especially in places far from where mangoes are grown.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Storing mangoes at 12°C (54°F) slows ripening and protects fruit quality, offering a practical way to keep mangoes fresh longer and reduce spoilage during transport.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent la température idéale pour conserver les mangues plus longtemps
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les mangues sont des fruits tropicaux appréciés pour leur goût sucré et leurs qualités nutritionnelles. Mais elles mûrissent rapidement après la récolte, ce qui les fait pourrir vite pendant le stockage et le transport.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Hainan ont découvert que conserver les mangues à 12°C ralentit leur maturation et les garde fraîches beaucoup plus longtemps, sans causer de dommages liés au froid. Ils ont comparé des mangues stockées à cette température avec celles conservées à environ 30°C pendant 24 jours.

**Impact** – La température plus basse a aidé les mangues à rester fermes, à garder leur humidité et à préserver des antioxydants importants comme la vitamine C. Elle a aussi activé les systèmes naturels de défense du fruit, protégeant les cellules des dégâts et ralentissant le ramollissement et la décoloration. Cela permet de transporter les mangues plus loin et de les conserver plus longtemps sans perdre leur qualité.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces résultats peuvent améliorer le transport et le stockage des mangues et autres fruits tropicaux dans le monde entier. L'industrie de la mangue pourrait adopter la conservation à 12°C pour réduire le gaspillage et livrer des fruits plus frais, surtout dans les régions éloignées des zones de production.

**Une phrase clé** – Conserver les mangues à 12°C ralentit leur maturation et protège leur qualité, offrant une méthode pratique pour prolonger leur fraîcheur et réduire la détérioration pendant le transport.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran la temperatura ideal para mantener las mangos frescos por más tiempo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los mangos son frutas tropicales populares por su sabor dulce y valor nutricional. Sin embargo, maduran rápido después de la cosecha, lo que hace que se echen a perder fácilmente durante el almacenamiento y transporte.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Hainan descubrieron que guardar los mangos a 12°C (54°F) ralentiza la maduración y mantiene la fruta fresca mucho más tiempo sin causar daños por frío. Compararon mangos almacenados a esta temperatura con otros guardados a cerca de 30°C durante 24 días.

**Impacto** – La temperatura más baja ayudó a que los mangos se mantuvieran firmes, conservaran la humedad y preservaran antioxidantes importantes como la vitamina C. También activó los sistemas naturales de defensa del fruto, protegiendo las células del daño y retrasando el ablandamiento y el cambio de color. Esto permite transportar los mangos a distancias más largas y almacenarlos más tiempo sin perder calidad.

**Próximo paso** – Estos hallazgos pueden mejorar el transporte y almacenamiento de mangos y otras frutas tropicales en todo el mundo. La industria del mango podría usar la conservación a 12°C para reducir desperdicios y entregar frutas más frescas, especialmente en mercados alejados de las zonas de cultivo.

**Una frase clave** – Guardar los mangos a 12°C ralentiza su maduración y protege su calidad, ofreciendo una forma práctica de mantenerlos frescos por más tiempo y reducir el deterioro durante el transporte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of ripe mangoes resting gently on a cool, softly glowing blue surface symbolizing 12°C storage, with subtle cellular patterns and delicate golden antioxidant light weaving through the fruit’s flesh, contrasting with a faint warm orange background representing faster ripening at higher temperatures.

articles.title: Rare coastal grassland restoration completed
articles.summary: Almost £1m is spent successfully restoring magnesian limestone grasslands in County Durham.
articles.content: "The project linked directly with schools and the wider community partners to enable people to help us create Tina's Haven and it has been a joy to witness the impact nature has played in supporting people through recovery while they support nature recovery."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a successful restoration of rare magnesian limestone grasslands with significant funding and community involvement, highlighting both environmental conservation and positive social impact through nature-supported recovery.
articles.personality_title: Rare coastal grasslands restored with community support in County Durham
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Magnesian limestone grasslands are a rare type of natural habitat found in only a few places, including County Durham in England. These grasslands are important because they support special plants and animals.

**What happened** – Nearly £1 million was spent to restore these rare grasslands in County Durham. The project involved local schools and community groups working together to create an area called Tina's Haven. People helped plant and care for the land over time.

**Impact** – This restoration not only brought back a rare natural habitat but also helped people in the community. Visitors and volunteers found that spending time in nature supported their recovery and well-being. The project showed how caring for the environment can also improve people’s lives.

**What's next step** – The team will continue to maintain Tina's Haven and encourage more community involvement. They hope this project inspires other places to restore natural habitats with local support.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A £1 million project in County Durham successfully restored rare grasslands while bringing people together to support both nature and community health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Restauration réussie d’une rare prairie côtière dans le comté de Durham
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les prairies de calcaire magnésien sont un type rare d’habitat naturel que l’on trouve seulement dans quelques endroits, notamment dans le comté de Durham en Angleterre. Ces prairies sont importantes car elles abritent des plantes et des animaux particuliers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Près d’un million de livres sterling ont été dépensés pour restaurer ces prairies rares dans le comté de Durham. Le projet a impliqué des écoles locales et des groupes communautaires qui ont travaillé ensemble pour créer un espace appelé Tina’s Haven. Les habitants ont aidé à planter et entretenir la terre.

**Impact** – Cette restauration a non seulement fait renaître un habitat naturel rare, mais elle a aussi aidé les habitants. Les visiteurs et bénévoles ont constaté que passer du temps dans la nature aidait à leur rétablissement et bien-être. Le projet montre comment prendre soin de l’environnement peut aussi améliorer la vie des gens.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe continuera à entretenir Tina’s Haven et encouragera une plus grande implication communautaire. Ils espèrent que ce projet inspirera d’autres lieux à restaurer des habitats naturels avec le soutien local.

**En une phrase** – Un projet d’un million de livres dans le comté de Durham a restauré avec succès des prairies rares tout en rassemblant la communauté pour soutenir la nature et la santé locale.
articles.personality_title_es: Restauración exitosa de raras praderas costeras en el condado de Durham
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las praderas de piedra caliza magnésica son un tipo raro de hábitat natural que se encuentra solo en algunos lugares, incluido el condado de Durham en Inglaterra. Estas praderas son importantes porque albergan plantas y animales especiales.

**Qué pasó** – Se gastaron casi 1 millón de libras para restaurar estas raras praderas en el condado de Durham. El proyecto involucró a escuelas locales y grupos comunitarios que trabajaron juntos para crear un área llamada Tina’s Haven. La gente ayudó a plantar y cuidar la tierra con el tiempo.

**Impacto** – Esta restauración no solo devolvió un hábitat natural raro, sino que también ayudó a las personas de la comunidad. Los visitantes y voluntarios encontraron que pasar tiempo en la naturaleza apoyaba su recuperación y bienestar. El proyecto mostró cómo cuidar el medio ambiente puede mejorar la vida de las personas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo continuará manteniendo Tina’s Haven y fomentará más participación comunitaria. Esperan que este proyecto inspire a otros lugares a restaurar hábitats naturales con apoyo local.

**Una frase para llevar** – Un proyecto de casi 1 millón de libras en el condado de Durham restauró con éxito praderas raras mientras unía a la comunidad para apoyar la naturaleza y la salud local.
articles.image_prompt: A softly colored, detailed painting of a gently rolling coastal grassland with diverse native grasses and wildflowers thriving, interspersed with abstract, glowing silhouettes of children and community figures formed from light, symbolizing their nurturing presence and connection to the land, all under a warm, serene sky that conveys healing and hope.
